parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 diff introduces significant new infrastructure (retry logic, BigModel error dispatch, job tracking, state management) with several material risks: (1) the new retry.mjs module has no protection against retry storms on non-idempotent write operations, (2) the BigModel error classifier silently swallows unrecognized error shapes and returns a generic retry-everything policy, (3) state.mjs uses file-based locking without stale-lock cleanup on crash, creating a deadlock vector, (4) the removeState/force-clear path in state.mjs has no confirmation guard and no backup, enabling irreversible state loss from a single misinvocation. These are the kind of failures that are expensive in production and hard to diagnose after the fact.

findings.title: retry.mjs retries non-idempotent operations without operation-awareness
findings.body: The retry module (scripts/lib/retry.mjs) applies blanket retry logic based on error classification from bigmodel-errors.mjs, but contains no mechanism to distinguish idempotent reads from non-idempotent writes or mutations. If a job submission or state-mutating call fails after the server processed it but before the response arrived, a retry will duplicate the operation. The retry policy is error-type-driven only, not operation-driven. This is a classic duplicate-submission vector under transient network failures.
findings.recommendation: Add an idempotency-key header to all mutating requests. Before retrying, verify the operation is safe to replay (e.g., check job state before re-submitting). At minimum, document which operations are safe to retry and which are not, and refuse to retry non-idempotent calls.

findings.title: bigmodel-errors.mjs silently defaults to retry on unrecognized error shapes
findings.body: The BigModel error classifier in scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs categorizes incoming errors to determine retry policy. When an error does not match any known pattern (e.g., a new API error code, a malformed response, or an unexpected HTTP status), the classifier falls through to a default that allows retry rather than failing fast. This means any novel failure mode—including auth revocation, account suspension, quota exhaustion with a new error code—will be retried until the retry budget is exhausted, delaying user visibility of the real problem and potentially making it worse (e.g., rate-limit escalation).
findings.recommendation: Invert the default: unknown errors should be classified as non-retryable (fail-fast) unless they explicitly match a known retryable pattern. Add logging when an unrecognized error shape is encountered so operators can diagnose and add coverage.

findings.title: state.mjs file-based locking has no stale-lock recovery on crash
findings.body: scripts/lib/state.mjs uses file-based state with what appears to be lockfile-based concurrency control. If the process crashes (OOM kill, SIGKILL, CI timeout) while holding the lock, the stale lockfile persists indefinitely, blocking all subsequent state operations for that user/project. There is no timeout-based lock acquisition, no PID validation, and no crash-recovery cleanup path. In CI environments especially, this creates a persistent deadlock that is invisible until someone manually inspects the filesystem.
findings.recommendation: Implement lock staleness detection: record the lock-holder PID and timestamp, and allow lock acquisition if the holder is dead or the lock exceeds a reasonable age (e.g., 60s). Add a --force-unlock CLI escape hatch with appropriate warnings. Test the crash-recovery path explicitly.

findings.title: removeState/force-clear has no confirmation guard or backup before irreversible deletion
findings.body: The state management path includes a force-clear or removeState operation that deletes persistent state files. There is no confirmation prompt, no backup of the state before deletion, and no undo mechanism. A single misinvocation—especially in scripted/CI contexts—destroys all tracked job state with no recovery path. Combined with the stale-lock issue above, users reaching for --force to clear a stuck lock may inadvertently destroy their entire state.
findings.recommendation: Before deleting state, write a backup to a timestamped sidecar file (e.g., .glm-state.bak.1718000000). Add a --confirm flag or interactive prompt for destructive operations in non-CI contexts. Document the recovery procedure.

next_steps: Audit retry.mjs call sites to identify which operations are non-idempotent and add idempotency guards or skip-retry annotations.
Change bigmodel-errors.mjs default classification from retryable to non-retryable for unrecognized error shapes, and add telemetry for unmatched errors.
Add stale-lock recovery to state.mjs with PID-based validation and age-based fallback, plus an integration test simulating crash-during-lock.
Implement pre-delete state backup in state.mjs before running any destructive state operations.
Re-run the UAT scenarios with simulated network failures and process kills to validate crash/retry safety end-to-end.
